chunk_summary: This section discusses discharge decisions in outpatient CMAM programs in India when MUAC and WHZ criteria for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) management disagree, referencing WHO, IAP, and NITI Aayog guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for discharge in CMAM programs when MUAC and WHZ measurements conflict.
Learn the role of WHO and national guidelines in managing SAM in outpatient settings.
Identify best practices for community-based management of SAM in India.
key_facts: MUAC (Mid-Upper Arm Circumference) <115 mm and WHZ (Weight-for-Height Z-score) <-3 are key diagnostic criteria for SAM.
Discharge decisions should prioritize clinical improvement and adherence to WHO and IAP guidelines.
The NITI Aayog report emphasizes community-based approaches for SAM management in India.
WHO guidelines (2023) recommend harmonizing MUAC and WHZ criteria to avoid premature discharge.
The 2007 UNICEF-WHO joint statement supports community-based management as a scalable solution for SAM.
reference_quote: "Discharge decisions should be based on clinical improvement and adherence to WHO and IAP guidelines to ensure sustainable recovery." (Source: https://iapindia.org/pdf/Ch-136-Severe-Acute-Malnutrition.pdf)
document_key_topics: Community-based management of SAM in India
chunk_heading: SEVERE ACUTE MALNUTRITION - (SAM) MANAGEMENT;OUTPATIENT CMAM IMPLEMENTATION IN INDIA;DISCHARGE DECISIONS WHEN MUAC AND WHZ DISAGREE;
sub_subtopic: DISCHARGE DECISIONS WHEN MUAC AND WHZ DISAGREE
